constructs: retrieval strength storage strength inhibition learning
claim: Temporary suppression of accessibility during memory competition creates conditions for accelerated relearning by reducing initial state and expanding potential gains.
Opportunity for direct feature comparison during learning strengthens encoding of distinctive features independent of temporal spacing effects.
Irrelevant sound disrupts retrieval processes that depend on semantic or category structure but not those driven by serial-order coding.
Semantic interference arises from filtering nontarget responses in peripheral output buffer rather than from competitive activation during word retrieval.
Skilled readers adjust reliance on phonological pathways based on stimulus category when context makes pathway adequacy predictable.
Semantic neighbors exert both inhibitory and facilitative effects simultaneously depending on feature-space distance, unified by attractor settling dynamics.
